structured.summary: This paper addresses the problem of adversarial robustness in fine-tuning pre-trained language models, identifying catastrophic forgetting as a key challenge. It proposes Robust Informative Fine-Tuning (RIFT), an information-theoretic method that encourages the fine-tuned model to retain features from the pre-trained model via maximizing conditional mutual information, and demonstrates its effectiveness on sentiment analysis and natural language inference tasks.

strengths.point: Novel perspective on catastrophic forgetting in adversarial fine-tuning
strengths.evidence: Section 2.2 discusses how adversarial fine-tuning intensifies catastrophic forgetting, a phenomenon not previously well-studied in this context.

strengths.point: Innovative use of conditional mutual information to retain pre-trained features
strengths.evidence: Section 2.3 and 2.4 introduce the information-theoretic objective maximizing I(S;T|Y) to better align with downstream tasks.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results demonstrating improved robustness
strengths.evidence: Section 4.3 shows RIFT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on IMDB and SNLI datasets under multiple adversarial attacks.

strengths.point: Comprehensive experimental evaluation with ablation studies
strengths.evidence: Section 4.5 provides ablation on the hyperparameter α and shows trade-offs between robustness and accuracy.

weaknesses.point: Limited scope of tasks and datasets
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments are conducted only on two NLP tasks (sentiment analysis and natural language inference) and two datasets (IMDB and SNLI), which may limit generalizability.
Section 4.5 notes that the contrastive loss complexity grows quadratically with batch size, which may hinder scalability.

weaknesses.point: Lack of detailed analysis on failure cases or limitations
weaknesses.evidence: While limitations are briefly mentioned in Section 4.5, there is no in-depth discussion of scenarios where RIFT might underperform.
Section 4.5 shows performance depends on α, but guidance on choosing α or robustness to its variation is limited.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation on other types of adversarial attacks beyond word substitutions
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 focuses exclusively on synonym-based word substitution attacks, leaving robustness to other attack types unexplored.

weaknesses.point: No runtime or efficiency comparison with baselines
weaknesses.evidence: Although Appendix A.3 is mentioned for runtime analysis, the main text lacks discussion on computational overhead introduced by RIFT.

weaknesses.point: Theoretical assumptions and proofs are deferred to appendix
weaknesses.evidence: Key theoretical results such as Lemma 1 are only proved in Appendix A.1, which may limit accessibility.

questions.question: How does RIFT perform on other NLP tasks beyond classification, such as sequence labeling or generation?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating generality across diverse NLP tasks would strengthen the impact and applicability of the method.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more insights or guidelines on selecting the hyperparameter α in practice?
questions.motivation: Since α controls the trade-off between retaining pre-trained features and fitting downstream data, practical guidance would aid adoption.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead of RIFT compared to standard adversarial fine-tuning, and how does it scale with dataset size?
questions.motivation: Understanding efficiency is important for real-world deployment, especially given the quadratic complexity of the contrastive loss.

questions.question: Have the authors considered combining RIFT with other robustness techniques, such as data augmentation or certified defenses?
questions.motivation: Exploring complementary methods could further improve robustness and provide a more comprehensive defense strategy.

critique_points.point_001: Limited scope of tasks and datasets (Experiments are conducted only on two NLP tasks (sentiment analysis and natural language inference) and two datasets (IMDB and SNLI), which may limit generalizability.)
critique_points.point_002: Computational complexity of contrastive loss (Section 4.5 notes that the contrastive loss complexity grows quadratically with batch size, which may hinder scalability.)
critique_points.point_003: Lack of detailed analysis on failure cases or limitations (While limitations are briefly mentioned in Section 4.5, there is no in-depth discussion of scenarios where RIFT might underperform.)
critique_points.point_004: Potential sensitivity to hyperparameter α (Section 4.5 shows performance depends on α, but guidance on choosing α or robustness to its variation is limited.)
critique_points.point_005: No evaluation on other types of adversarial attacks beyond word substitutions (Section 4.1 focuses exclusively on synonym-based word substitution attacks, leaving robustness to other attack types unexplored.)
critique_points.point_006: No runtime or efficiency comparison with baselines (Although Appendix A.3 is mentioned for runtime analysis, the main text lacks discussion on computational overhead introduced by RIFT.)
critique_points.point_007: Theoretical assumptions and proofs are deferred to appendix (Key theoretical results such as Lemma 1 are only proved in Appendix A.1, which may limit accessibility.)
critique_points.point_008: How does RIFT perform on other NLP tasks beyond classification, such as sequence labeling or generation? (Demonstrating generality across diverse NLP tasks would strengthen the impact and applicability of the method.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the authors provide more insights or guidelines on selecting the hyperparameter α in practice? (Since α controls the trade-off between retaining pre-trained features and fitting downstream data, practical guidance would aid adoption.)
critique_points.point_010: What is the computational overhead of RIFT compared to standard adversarial fine-tuning, and how does it scale with dataset size? (Understanding efficiency is important for real-world deployment, especially given the quadratic complexity of the contrastive loss.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered combining RIFT with other robustness techniques, such as data augmentation or certified defenses? (Exploring complementary methods could further improve robustness and provide a more comprehensive defense strategy.)
